The reading topped all estimates… Non-revolving credit, such as loans for vehicle purchases and school tuition, increased $10.2 billion in December, the most in seven months. Meantime, credit card and other revolving debt outstanding jumped $13.8 billion, the biggest monthly gain in over two years."

February 12 - CNBC (Diana Olick): "High home prices, faltering supply and weaker consumer confidence in the economy all continue to weigh on the U.S. housing market. The chief economist for the National Association of Realtors, Lawrence Yun, is calling it 'a new housing crisis.' Sales of previously owned homes in January dropped a much wider-than-expected 8.4% from December to a… annualized rate of 3.91 million... Sales were 4.4% lower than January 2025. That is the slowest pace since December 2023 and the biggest monthly drop since February 2022… Inventory came down in January from December but was still up 3.4% year over year. There were 1.22 million homes for sale at the end of January, which at the current sales pace is a 3.7-month supply. A six-month supply is considered a balanced market…"

February 9 - Axios (Sami Sparber): "U.S. homeowners are staying in their houses for the longest time in at least 25 years… That - along with still-high home prices and tight inventory - is keeping the housing market on ice. Sellers at the end of 2025 had owned their homes for an average of 8.6 years - a record in data going back to early 2000… Homeowner tenure has increased steadily in almost every major metro area over the past two decades, according to ATTOM... The 'trend is especially pronounced in coastal and Northeast metros, where tenure often exceeds a decade, while many Sun Belt and Midwest markets continue to see comparatively shorter ownership periods,' CEO Rob Barber tells Axios."

China Watch:

February 9 - Bloomberg (Nectar Gan, Shawna Kwan and Laura Davison): "A Hong Kong court sentenced former media mogul Jimmy Lai to 20 years in prison, handing the pro-democracy advocate the heaviest penalty ever meted out under a Beijing-imposed national security law. Three judges hand-picked by the government… ordered the 78-year-old to effectively spend the rest of his life behind bars for two counts of conspiring to collude with foreign forces and a separate sedition conviction. 'Lai was no doubt the mastermind of all three conspiracies charged and therefore he warrants a heavier sentence,' the judges said..."

February 8 - Bloomberg: "The People's Bank of China is boosting the supply of money available to banks to ensure they can meet the surge in demand for cash during the Lunar New Year holidays. The central bank injected a total of 600 billion yuan ($86.4bn) via 14-day repurchase agreements late last week… Industrial Securities forecasts the PBOC to add as much as 3.5 trillion yuan of funds via similar tools before the holidays kick off on Sunday."

February 9 - Bloomberg: "Chinese government bonds' recent recovery is drawing more traders into derivatives, reflecting stronger demand for safety and hedging as confidence in the debt market grows. The number of open futures contracts tied to China's 10‑year government bonds rose on Monday to 344,171, the highest since they started trading in 2015… That's a 46% jump since the beginning of the year, outpacing the 24% rise seen in all of 2025."

February 10 - CNBC (Anniek Bao): "China's consumer inflation rose less than expected in January… The consumer price index rose 0.2% in January from a year earlier…, below economists' forecast of 0.4% increase... That followed a 0.8% growth in December, its highest level in nearly three years. Prices rose 0.2% month-on-month, below economists' forecast of a 0.3% increase. Core CPI, which strips out volatile food and energy prices, jumped 0.8% from a year earlier, easing from the 1.2% in December."

Central Banker Watch:

February 10 - Wall Street Journal (James Glynn): "Australia's inflation rate is too high and the costs of allowing it to remain elevated don't bear thinking about, RBA's Deputy Gov. Andrew Hauser said… 'That's we acted last week to raise the official cash rate by 25 bps, and why I wanted to say we will continue to do to do whatever is necessary to ensure that inflation does return to the target band,' he told a business luncheon. The rise in the official cash rate last week reversed a cut delivered in August… Both core and headline inflation have moved above the RBA's 2% to 3% target band… The RBA decided to reverse the direction of monetary policy from easing last year to tightening this month because 'the facts changed' on several fronts, with the strength of the world economy a key factor, he said. 'I don't think anyone thought that we would be sitting here in 2026 with the global economy powering ahead,' he said."

Europe Watch:

February 10 - Financial Times (Euan Healy): "Billionaire Andrea Pignataro's heavily indebted fintech empire is under increasing pressure in credit markets, as concerns over AI's impact on software companies hit one of Europe's largest sellers of junk bonds. The sell-off in bonds issued by Ion Group, a debt-fuelled roll-up of financial data companies including Mergermarket, Fidessa and Dealogic, has accelerated in the past week…"

February 12 - Bloomberg (Zoltan Simon): "Hungarian opposition leader Peter Magyar said he was secretly taped having sex with his former partner in 2024, preempting a potential release of a video that he called a 'Russia-style' attempt to blackmail him before pivotal elections in April."

EM Watch:

February 12 - Bloomberg (Zijia Song): "Bitcoin's crash has hit few places as hard as El Salvador, exposing the risks of President Nayib Bukele's high-stakes embrace of crypto and whipsawing the country's debt markets. Bukele, an ardent advocate who made the token legal tender alongside the dollar, has kept buying one Bitcoin a day even as the latest rout erased hundreds of millions of dollars from the government's holdings and complicated talks with the International Monetary Fund over a $1.4 billion loan. The focus is now catching up with him in financial markets, where investors have pushed credit default swaps to the highest level in five months, signaling growing unease over the country's crypto-heavy strategy."

February 10 - Bloomberg (Vinicius Andrade and Giovanna Bellotti Azevedo): "It's just two companies. But their escalating financial strains have sent bondholders racing to the exits and sown fear that more Brazilian businesses will be driven to the brink by the nation's highest interest rates in two decades. The worries were thrown into sharp relief on Monday, when Raízen SA was hit by back-to-back downgrades that knocked it from investment grade to deep into junk status… The reaction was swift: Investors unloaded the securities, extending a selloff that's slashed the price of some bonds nearly in half over the past week to around 46 cents on the dollar."

Leveraged Speculation Watch:

February 8 - Bloomberg (Iris Ouyang): "China's low-interest rate environment is driving a surge in interbank borrowing, as financial institutions take advantage of cheap funding to buy bonds. The daily volume of overnight repurchase contracts rose to a record 8.2 trillion yuan ($1.2 trillion) on Friday… Financial institutions are increasingly using this tool to fund leveraged bond purchases. Trading has surged after the People's Bank of China injected liquidity, driving interbank borrowing costs to multi-year lows as it cushions banks against higher demand for cash during the Lunar New Year holidays."

Social, Political, Environmental and Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

February 11 - Bloomberg (Mark Gongloff): "The Trump administration will soon make it the official policy of the US government that greenhouse gases don't endanger Americans' well-being and therefore don't need federal regulation. Insurance companies, meanwhile, live in a parallel universe where greenhouse gases are heating the atmosphere and intensifying natural disasters, harming human health, destroying property and raising insurance costs. The US government's universe is an increasingly lonely fantasy world. You're trapped in the real one. The oxymoronically named Environmental Protection Agency will this week formally renounce its 2009 'endangerment finding,' which gives it regulatory power over emissions from cars, power plants, factories and more. This move, which the climate-change deniers running the White House call a 'total victory,' is for now mostly symbolic."

February 7 - Axios (Nathan Bomey): "Gambling culture is enveloping American sports, politics, media and trading, bringing betting out of the shadows and into the mainstream in a way that disturbs some and exhilarates others. What was once a fringe vice is fast becoming a mass-market habit - raising urgent questions about addiction, fairness and who should regulate the business of betting on almost anything. 'Wanna bet on that?' That age-old contemplation has become more realistic than ever with the explosion of online sportsbooks and prediction markets… The American Gaming Association projects that $1.7 billion will be legally wagered on the Super Bowl… News media orgs like CNBC, CNN, Yahoo Finance and Dow Jones have struck partnerships to showcase prediction market data to viewers and readers."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.description:
Rose’s Income Garden “RIG” portfolio contains 75 dividend-paying investments in 11 sectors, where 47+ have investment-grade S&P credit ratings. The remainder of those not rated are financials, preferred shares, or ETFs. The portfolio has a forward yield of 6.09%, with most of the financials and ETFs offering the highest yields, but again, they are mostly rarely rated. This article is about some of the highly S&P-rated ones with nice higher yields that might just interest you to purchase.

8 Quality S&P rated companies with 4.7% - 10% Yield

The first 2 charts are a review for:

1- Dividend % Yield

2- Price to Earnings or Funds from Operations

Lastly comes the company information, charts, and Rose's recommendation.

The companies in the first chart below are presented from the highest to lowest yield.

The following abbreviations were used:

S&P = Standard & Poor's credit rating obtained from FAST Graphs "FG"

Curr Pr = Current $ price on February 13, 2026.

1- Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC) - 9.95% yield

This business development company/BDC specializes in growth capital primarily in the USA for middle-market companies. It is headquartered in LA, CA, and was founded in 2004. It has a BBB S&P credit rating, which is somewhat rare for a BDC to be rated at all. It has maintained a $1.92 dividend since 2023 and on occasion will offer special dividends but has not done so recently. It has announced the same quarterly dividend of 48c with an ex-date of March 13th and a pay date of the 30th.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The “SA” chart indicates a forward P/E of ~10x, making it close to a buy by that metric. It has a price/book of 0.98% from M* as of today and therefore is just below fair value.

Rose's recommendation

At a 10% yield or more is when I get interested in buying more.

It is a good value at a Goldilocks price right now along with having a quality S&P credit rating.

2- Altria Group, Inc. (MO) - 6.33% yield

This consumer staple worldwide company that offers oral and smokeable tobacco and nicotine products was founded in 1822 and is headquartered in Richmond, VA. It has a 5-year DGR of 4.12% and an S&P credit rating of BBB+. It pays quarterly and just raised the dividend by 4c to $1.06 or 2.9%, goes ex-dividend next in March for payment in April. It has paid rising dividends now for 56 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The P/E is over normal along with the M* fair value. Earnings estimates from FG suggest 3-4% up over the next few years. Not very exciting, and that could account for the lower 2.9% pay raise.

Rose's recommendation

It is quality but has risen in price quickly, and I would wait for a dip to add more, but the over 6% yield is tempting if you don’t own it.

3- VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) - 6.2% yield

VICI Properties Inc. is considered a casino real estate REIT, is headquartered in NY, NY, and has a BBB- S&P credit rating.

From Yahoo Finance in Dec 2025:

“It owns one of the largest portfolios of market-leading gaming, hospitality, wellness, entertainment, and leisure destinations, including Caesars Palace Las Vegas, MGM Grand, and the Venetian Resort Las Vegas, three of the most iconic entertainment facilities on the Las Vegas Strip. VICI Properties owns 93 experiential assets across a geographically diverse portfolio consisting of 54 gaming properties and 39 other experiential properties across the United States and Canada.”

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The statistics I show are for P/AFFO, which is from “FG” and differs from P/FFO shown above, but the bottom line is that it is currently undervalued. Earnings are looking to rise by ~3% this year and 2027 using FG estimates. It has a 5 year DGR of 7.08%, and it just raised it to 45c in September 2025 from 43.3%, or 3.9%, so the raise amount is on the downtrend.

Rose's recommendation

I like it under $30, better near $28. Note that M* has a higher price target, making it still a buy, and if a 6.1% yield is attractive for your portfolio, then it's time to buy some. I am waiting for another dip to add on in RIG.

4 - Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) - 5.68% yield

This worldwide communications company, formerly known as Bell Atlantic, incorporated in 1983 and changed its name in 2000. It provides technology, information, and entertainment products and is headquartered in NY, NY. It has a 5-year DGR of ~2% and a record of raising the dividend for 21 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

It reliably pays quarterly, announcing a raise in October from 67.75c to 69c, or 1.8%, which was just paid most recently on February 2nd. It just announced an early raise to 70.75c, or 2.5%, to go ex April 10th and be paid May 1st. Surprise, surprise, and that caused its price to pop as well. M* has it undervalued with a 4* rating, suggesting it is still a buy with an FV of $53.

Rose's recommendation

This quality, reliable income stock has now gotten just a bit pricey, with the yield down to 5.7%. My PT is $47.25 even with the new yearly raise to $2.83 yearly. That price gives it a 6% yield, which is the minimum I want it to be, just for me.

5- British American Tobacco (BTI) - 5.18% yield

This consumer staple company that offers tobacco and nicotine products worldwide was founded in 1902 and is headquartered in London, UK. It has a 5-year DGR of 1.86% and a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It pays quarterly, with the latest being 81.3c, which was just paid on February 9th. It did just announce a full year of dividends with a raise to 61.26p, or 84c if I converted it correctly. I got the information from the following article in “SA” Feb. 12th. “

The actual amount received will vary according to the foreign exchange rate.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The normal P/E is a lot lower than it is now, as this stock was terribly undervalued for multiple years and is finally catching up to what might actually be considered more normal. Earnings are estimated to rise by 7-8% for the next 2 years, and it could be that the price is catching up with those expectations.

Rose's recommendation

It was purchased for RIG a long time ago at quite a nice lower price and quite a higher yield. I personally believe it is now overvalued but remain pleased with the new dividend announcement and hold it.

6- NNN REIT (NNN) - 5.5% yield

This is a retail triple net REIT that operates in 48 states and has headquarters in Orlando, FL, with a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It has a 5 year DGR of 2.66% and a record of raising dividends for 36 years. It pays quarterly 60c and went ex on Jan. 30th, which will be paid on Feb. 13th.

The current P/AFFO is 12.47x, and the norm is higher at 13.84x, which indicates it is somewhat undervalued.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

Rose's recommendation

Quality, reliable dividend payer with a nice, fair, rather decent yield. It could still have some capital gains, as M* gives it a price target of $49.21. I believe it is fair value and would want more cheaper for my RIG portfolio, and a 6% yield would be down near a $40 price, with my fingers crossed for that to happen.

7- W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) - 5.12% yield

WP Carey is a large international industrial triple net real estate REIT headquartered in NY., NY. It has a BBB+ S&P credit rating. It operates offices in NY, London, Amsterdam, and Dallas, TX.

Quarterly payer WP Carey usually raises its dividend each quarter, not yearly as most others do. The important takeaway is that the price has been rising, and one could say catching up to where it had been once upon a time. The yield is holding steady above 5% with the most recent raises. I and many other investors lost confidence in management when it split off the office portion of its portfolio without adequate warning and notice of a dividend cut coming as well.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The current P/AFFO is 14.3x, and the normal is 13.80x, which makes it just a bit overvalued using that metric. Earnings are suggested to grow 3-4% over the next 2 years using FG.

Rose's recommendation

I believe this quality company is now overvalued, with the yield trending down faster than desired. I will look to perhaps trim it if the price rises up to near $75 -80.

8- Kimberly-Clark Corp. (KMB) - 4.73% yield

Kimberly-Clark Corp., in the consumer staple sector, provides household and personal care products, is headquartered in Dallas, TX, and was founded in 1872. It has an "A" S&P credit rating while being a dividend champion/aristocrat that has paid rising dividends for 54 years.

5-year Seeking Alpha Technical Chart

The normal P/E is 19.68 and it currently sells at 13.84x, being undervalued using that metric.

Earnings are expected to rise slowly at 1-2% over the next 2 years, which could be the reason for some of that lower price. The January dividend of $1.26 was paid on the 5th. It just announced the next quarterly dividend and a raise of 2c to $1.28. It is disappointing but does go along with the lower estimated earnings.

Rose's recommendation

For a non-tobacco consumer staple, this is still quite a high yield with 3 dividends left for 2026. This is just a nice quality company with a reliable dividend and hopefully some capital gains coming in the future to match the M* fair value of $133. In full disclosure, I did just add at the 5% yield and would do so again if there is a dip.

Summary/Conclusion

RIG, with its 75 investments, is hitting all-time highs for portfolio value and is up 6.7% YTD. For today, while maintaining a 6.05% forward yield. It is also beating SPY by 6.14% since its inception in November 2021.

data.title: Operational Progress, Macro Headwinds Drive Resideo Technologies
data.description:
Stronger Platform, Softer End Markets

Resideo Technologies (REZI), which provides comfort, energy management, and safety and security solutions, displays an interesting journey. It has scaled its B2B platform by leveraging customer stickiness. It taps into the Connected Home growth engine by introducing a range of high-value, differentiated products in recent times. It has streamlined its operations with the exit of the non-core Grid Services and the elimination of the legacy Honeywell liabilities.

But the focus on margins also highlights the lack of a compelling underlying investment option. The HVAC market is going through a disruption (transitioning to new low-GWP refrigerants). This brought out inventory issues that are typically cyclical and timing-related. On a positive note, the cash flow problem appears to be temporary. In my view, the management can resolve it with the completion of the indemnification payment. The stock’s current valuation multiples appear reasonable compared to its peers. So, I recommend a “Hold,” unchanged from my previous iteration.

Housing Market And Inventory Challenges

The macro factors may not be in REZI’s favor, but the current trend indicates the wind may return. By late 2025, I see indications that the new residential homebuilding lacks firepower, which will stifle the repair and remodeling market. The U.S. mortgage rate stretched out over the past year. In the past year until October 2025, the privately owned housing units in the US decreased by ~8%, while the US mortgage rate (30-year average) expanded by more than 200 basis points. However, since October, mortgage rates have declined.

The softness in the Residential housing and HVAC (heating, ventilation, and air conditioning) markets disrupted REZI’s demand and inventory. It led to refrigerant-related inventory issues late in Q3. It is generally observed that the U.S. HVAC industry is transitioning to low-GWP refrigerants under new regulations. So, the legacy HVAC equipment stockpile slowed new demand. However, its management sees the HVAC disruption as temporary. It expects normalization following policy clarity over the next few quarters.

M&A and Other Strategies

REZI’s management sounded confident in the Q3 earnings call about achieving profitable growth through differentiated Connected Home products. Investors may note that Resideo primarily operates through two segments - Products & Solutions and ADI Global Distribution. Its expansion in security and residential AV (audio-visual) should bring the desired scale to its operations. In this context, I discussed the benefits of a combined Rezi and Snap One. The deal was struck in June 2024 for $1.4 billion. Subsequently, REZI’s customer engagement and cross-sales opportunities increased through AI adoption.

The evidence of REZI’s focus on core operations is found in its disposal of the Grid Services demand response business in December. The divestiture will enable its home energy management through third-party platform integrations.

In the ADI segment, I expect a robust e-commerce execution to strengthen its competitive positioning. For example, the company’s Omnichannel B2B platform is a fully integrated digital and physical distribution model. Also note that ADI serves more than 100,000 professional customers annually. For this segment, e-commerce can play a significant role in order placement, product discovery, and fulfillment efficiency.

Q4 and FY2025 Outlook

According to REZI’s forecast, its revenues can increase marginally (at the guidance mid-point) in Q4 2025 compared to Q3. Its adjusted EBITDA is expected to increase by 2%. It can also generate an EPS in the range of $0.42–$0.52.

In FY2025, REZI expects to record $7.45 billion in revenue (at the guidance mid-point), a 10% rise compared to FY2024. It raised cash from operations guidance to $410–$450 million. The company will likely record an adjusted EBITDA of $818M–$832M.

My Estimates

Over the past 20 quarters, REZI’s adjusted EBITDA increased by 3% on average. I do not expect growth to rebound sharply in the short term because the macroeconomic factors are not bullish, and the headwinds from HVAC inventory disruptions can adversely affect margin. However, new product categories and the impact of the acquisitions will slowly translate into improved revenue stream and higher margins. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 2%-4%.

Risks and Catalysts

So, I think two factors can heavily influence REZI’s Q1 2026 numbers – mortgage rates and HVAC inventory movement. If the mortgage rate reverses, as recent indicators show (the average 30-year mortgage rate declined by 6 basis points), it will support housing turnover and remodeling activity. This can lead to a higher demand for the company’s services/products and improve its margin. On the other hand, if the HVAC inventory continues to accumulate before it eases, sales growth will be deferred.

My estimate is that the REZI’s revenue and EBITDA numbers in Q1 will track the Q4’05 figures. Its quarter-over-quarter revenue growth was 2%, on average, for the past 20 quarters. It can improve to ~3% in Q1. Adjusted EBITDA can stick to the 3% average in Q1.

Analyzing Q3 Segment Results

As disclosed in the earnings report released on November 5, the company's operating segments posted a strong margin growth despite a modest revenue rise. Robust demand for new products like First Alert connected smoke, ElitePRO smart thermostat, and CO (carbon monoxide) detectors drove its performance in Q3. The improvement was distributed across its sales channels, including Retail, OEM, and electrical distribution channels. Both volume and prices improved in Q3.

Ordinarily, higher operating income translates to an increased cash flow from operations. But the chain broke for REZI. In fact, cash flow from operations turned significantly negative ($1.4 billion) in 9M 2025 compared to a positive cash flow a year ago.

The dip was tied to a non-recurring cash outflow from the Honeywell indemnification termination. In August, REZI paid $1.59 billion in cash to Honeywell to terminate a long-standing Indemnification Agreement. The termination of the agreement marks REZI’s elimination of contingent risk, simplification of its structure, and gaining full financial independence from Honeywell. Because the indemnification payment was partly funded with borrowing, the company’s debt/equity (leverage) ratio deteriorated as of September 30, 2025. Investors may note that Resideo was spun off from Honeywell in 2018.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

REZI appears to be reasonably valued compared to its peers. Why? Because the rise in the forward EV/EBITDA multiple (versus the TTM ratio) suggests a premium over its competitors. Its TTM EV/EBITDA ratio (10.7x) falls short of the competitors’ CSW Industrials (CSW), Griffon Corporation (GFF), and Hayward Holdings (HAYW) average. However, the current multiple is higher than its five-year average multiple of 9.7x. So, I think, its relative valuation is within an acceptable range.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect a modest 2%—5% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and assuming the forward EV/EBITDA multiple holds, the stock can trade between $39 and $41, implying a ~10% upside in the near term. In calculating the expected EV, I have also adjusted excess liquidity, defined as total liquidity less three months’ operating costs. I estimate that it has an excess liquidity of $443 million.

I think the stock’s 32% jump over the past six months has put off the returns potential despite the strong long-term drivers. Of course, the near-term margin pressure can put off investors’ enthusiasm. So, the sell-side analysts’ 24% expected returns seem overly optimistic.

What’s The Take On REZI?

I think REZI is navigating a soft housing and HVAC market. Therefore, it pursues discipline rather than chasing growth. The ongoing housing weakness and refrigerant-related inventory disruption have weakened the near-term demand. Cash flows going into the negative territory in 9M 2025 can ring an alarm bell for investors. But the issue relates to a past indemnification termination and has very limited bearing on its current operation. So, it should be resolved going forward.

However, these issues appear temporary. I think the company’s foundation has remained robust. It has kept its focus on Connected Home, security, and AV products. Its ADI segment’s e-commerce adoption helped smooth out execution. The company’s operating margin expansion in Q3 proves that its strategies have been working well. Its balance sheet is overleveraged. Also, the stock appears overvalued compared to its peers. So, I maintain my “Hold” call.
